title: Control Hierarchy & Implementation
description: The hierarchy of controls and practical implementation strategies for laser safety in entertainment environments.

phases.title: Hierarchy of Controls
phases.content: Effective laser safety follows the hierarchy of controls: Elimination/Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → Personal Protective Equipment. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for designing effective safety systems and passing certification examinations.

phases.title: Control Measure Categories
phases.content:
## Level 1: Elimination / Substitution
- Use lower power lasers when possible
- Position beams away from occupied areas
- Design shows to avoid audience exposure

## Level 2: Engineering Controls
- **Protective housings** - Enclose beam paths
- **Safety interlocks** - Prevent access during operation
- **Scan failure detection** - Stop emission if scanners fail
- **Emergency stops** - Immediate shutdown capability
- **Beam attenuators/shutters** - Control beam output
- **Remote interlock connectors** - External safety integration

## Level 3: Administrative Controls
- **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**
- **Training programs** - Initial and refresher training
- **Warning signs and labels**
- **Access restriction** - Controlled areas
- **Alignment procedures** - Safe maintenance protocols

## Level 4: Personal Protective Equipment
- **Protective eyewear** - Appropriate OD for wavelength and power
- **Protective clothing** - For high-power applications
- **Last resort** - Only when other controls insufficient

phases.title: Control Selection Exercise
phases.content:
For each scenario below, identify the most appropriate control measures following the hierarchy:

**Scenario A**: Class 4 laser in research laboratory with multiple users

**Scenario B**: Entertainment venue with audience-scanning laser effects

**Scenario C**: Medical laser suite in hospital operating room

**Scenario D**: Industrial manufacturing with automated laser welding

Consider which controls are most effective and why lower-level controls may be insufficient.

keyTakeaways: Elimination/substitution is always the most effective control level
Engineering controls physically isolate people from hazards
Administrative controls change how people work through procedures
PPE is the last line of defense, not a substitute for other controls
